channel.title: Alpha Funnel — News
channel.description: Daily News briefings from Alpha Funnel.

item.title: Bessent declared America will write the rules of money, four nuclear startups made Trump's July 4 reactor deadline, and a lone voice says the Iran relief is mispriced
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Treasury Secretary's speech on digital money drew a rare El-Erian endorsement while Circle collected a federal bank charter. The advanced-nuclear race quietly hit its milestone, the Kremlin floated talks while reportedly preparing to escalate, and China stuck its first reusable rocket landing. Here is the evening read.
item.content:encoded:
The Treasury Secretary's speech on digital money drew a rare El-Erian endorsement while Circle collected a federal bank charter. The advanced-nuclear race quietly hit its milestone, the Kremlin floated talks while reportedly preparing to escalate, and China stuck its first reusable rocket landing. Here is the evening read.

This morning the story was the Iran panic unwinding. Tonight the bigger story is structural, Washington telling you in plain language where money is headed, while the nuclear buildout the AI trade depends on hit a real milestone. Here is what matters, ranked.

1. Bessent's money speech is the policy document of the week, and the plumbing moved with it. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent laid out five principles, and per Jordi Visser the one that counts is the third, America will write the rules of the next economy. His own words: digital assets, stablecoins, tokenization, and new payment systems will help shape the future of money, and the United States should not consign itself to the sidelines while that future is built elsewhere. Mohamed El-Erian called it a remarkably important speech in a New York Times op-ed, and when the establishment's favorite economist blesses a crypto speech, the Overton window has moved.

The plumbing moved with the words. Per the Market list, Circle won final approval from the OCC (the national bank regulator) for a federally regulated trust bank to sit under USDC. The words and the charters are now pointing the same direction.

The test comes fast. The Senate returns July 13 with about three working weeks before the August recess, and per Milk Road that is the last realistic 2026 window for the CLARITY Act, the bill deciding whether the SEC or the CFTC regulates crypto, with Polymarket odds at 40 percent and falling. A government that says it will write the rules still has to pass one.

2. The oil market is treating the Iran pause as a done deal, and that is the vulnerable assumption. A Prof G Markets guest made the uncomfortable case against this morning's relief: an enormous amount of Middle East geopolitical risk is simply not priced into oil, markets are planning for the best case, and nothing about an unstable region with no functioning diplomacy justifies that. Twenty-four hours ago the tape was pricing a wider war, today it prices a handshake, and both cannot be right. The honest position is that a pause in strikes is a data point, and crude at best-case pricing has asymmetric room to reprice if Iran's phone call turns out to be a stall.

3. Four nuclear startups hit the July 4 deadline, and the customer is the data center. Per Packy McCormick, Aalo Atomics' reactor went critical at 12:20am on July 4, making it the fourth advanced-nuclear startup to hit the executive-order deadline, after Antares Nuclear, Valar Atomics, and Deployable Energy, with Radiant, Oklo, X-Energy, Deep Fission, and Last Energy expected next. The Abundance Institute's Chris Koopman puts the speed in one line, 13 months from executive orders to four critical reactors. The field has shifted from science project to manufacturing race, and the buyers driving it are data centers rather than utilities.

The supporting cast is forming around that same customer. Josh Zoffer argued in the FT that hyperscaler demand is now doing more than subsidies or tariffs to rebuild American industrial supply chains, and American Turbine came out of stealth under John McElhone to sell small, mass-manufacturable gas turbines aimed at data-center speed-to-power. Energy for compute is becoming its own industrial economy, and it is being built on deadlines measured in months.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Russia. Per the Market list, the Kremlin answered a Reuters report that Putin is likely to escalate the Ukraine war by saying Russia remains open to talks. Read the two together: the openness is the message, the escalation is the plan.
  • China's space program. Per the same list, China landed a reusable rocket for the first time, the capability that made SpaceX cheap. The gap is still years, but it just stopped being categorical.
  • Congressional trading. Rep. Richard McCormick, who sits on House Armed Services, filed a purchase of defense contractor L3Harris, per the Market list. The committee-to-portfolio pipeline remains undefeated.
  • Musk, at maximum Musk. Per the Market list, after unveiling an orbital AI-compute program, he posted that SpaceX will be worth more than the rest of Earth if it accomplishes its goals. File under ambition as pricing strategy.

item.title: The US strikes on Iran appear to have stopped overnight, Trump says Iran called to make a deal, and the panic that spiked oil yesterday is unwinding
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A senior US official says no strikes have happened in recent hours, gold and crude slid on fresh US-Iran optimism, and Japan's bond market reversed with the yen soaring. At home the Maine Senate primary imploded as Graham Platner moved to withdraw. Here is the overnight update.
item.content:encoded:
A senior US official says no strikes have happened in recent hours, gold and crude slid on fresh US-Iran optimism, and Japan's bond market reversed with the yen soaring. At home the Maine Senate primary imploded as Graham Platner moved to withdraw. Here is the overnight update.

Yesterday ended with Trump calling the ceasefire dead and the US resuming strikes. Overnight it turned the other way. The bombing appears to have paused, the language shifted to a deal, and the markets that priced a wider war started pricing a pause. At home, the messiest Senate primary in the country lost its front-runner. Here is the update.

1. The strikes paused and the tone flipped to a deal. The reversal was fast. Per the Polymarket list, a senior American official told Axios that no US military strikes have been conducted in recent hours, walking back yesterday afternoon's resumption. Per the Financial Markets list, the shift was strong enough to move prices overnight, with gold hesitating above $4,100 and heading for a weekly loss on less hawkish Fed minutes and fresh US-Iran optimism. The oil-shock story is being challenged directly, with analyst Anas Alhajji arguing the Strait of Hormuz supply loss was overstated once SPR releases and softer demand are netted out. The caution is that a pause is not a deal. Per the Kalshi list, traders still put only a 30 percent chance on a US-Iran nuclear agreement this year, near a record low.

2. The Maine Senate primary imploded overnight. The Democratic front-runner is out. Per the Kalshi list, Graham Platner is withdrawing, with his advisor saying he will file the paperwork and Axios reporting he told staff he will exit Monday, and the market puts an 87 percent chance he is gone before Tuesday. The race to replace him is now Troy Jackson at 49 percent, Shenna Bellows at 31 percent after officially announcing, and Nirav Shah at 14 percent, ahead of the July 31 primary. It is a clean reset of a seat that looked locked a week ago.

3. A Tea Party is brewing inside the Democratic primaries. The bigger political signal is nationwide. Per the Kalshi list, Harry Enten frames it as a new Tea Party for Democrats, with an 82 percent chance that six or more incumbent House Democrats lose their primaries. The named vulnerable seats are Missouri's Wesley Bell at a 38 percent chance of losing, Michigan's Shri Thanedar at 19 percent, and Connecticut's John Larson under 50 percent to hold. Separately, Rahm Emanuel is delivering remarks in Israel and carries an 80 percent chance he runs for president, and Pennsylvania's John Fetterman and Dave McCormick, a Democrat and a Republican, launched a joint fundraising committee called Common Ground PA.

4. Japan's bond market reversed, and it moved money. The macro tell came out of Tokyo. Per the Financial Markets list, the 10-year JGB yield fell 10 basis points to 2.775 percent and the 20-year fell 10 basis points to 3.765 percent, reversing yesterday's push to a 30-year high, as the yen soared on a pledge by Katayama to promote GPIF investment in Japanese assets. About $174 billion was added to the Japanese stock market at the open. After months of the JGB selloff being the global bond-stress story, an overnight reversal of that size is its own signal.

5. Around the world, quickly.

  • Ukraine. Per the Polymarket list, Zelensky says the number of countries wanting Ukraine in NATO has increased, a counter to yesterday's read that Putin was hardening against talks.
  • Europe's energy squeeze. Per the Polymarket list, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte warned that Europe is closing more refineries than it is opening, tying military readiness to fuel supply.
  • Canada. Per the Kalshi list, the economy shrank in Mark Carney's first year as prime minister, called the worst economic start for any Canadian PM in over 60 years.
  • The US consumer. Per the Kalshi list, consumer credit fell $182 million in May, the first monthly decline since November 2024, a small but real crack in the borrowing story.

item.title: The 1am ceasefire is dead, Trump said so on camera, and the US went back to bombing Iran while Khamenei was being buried
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: This morning's deal talk lasted hours. By afternoon Trump called it over and called Iran scum, oil ripped 7 percent toward $80, Jordan and Kuwait were intercepting Iranian missiles, and Maine's Senate seat flipped hard to the Democrats. Here is what moved since the open.
item.content:encoded:
This morning's deal talk lasted hours. By afternoon Trump called it over and called Iran scum, oil ripped 7 percent toward $80, Jordan and Kuwait were intercepting Iranian missiles, and Maine's Senate seat flipped hard to the Democrats. Here is what moved since the open.

The morning ended with Trump saying Iran wanted a deal. The afternoon ended with Trump saying the war was over because he was done talking to them. In between, the US struck Iran again, missiles flew at three more countries, and Iran buried its supreme leader. At home, the messiest Senate race in the country swung to the other party. Here is the update.

1. Trump killed the ceasefire on the record and the bombing resumed. The reversal was his own. Per Prof G Markets, asked directly if the ceasefire was over, Trump said, to me I think it's over, I don't want to deal with them anymore, they're scum, and if they had a nuclear weapon they'd use it. The US launched another round of strikes after. Brent oil soared more than 7 percent toward $80, the highest in over two weeks. Brian Katulis of the Middle East Institute put the frame on it, that the war Trump said would take four or five weeks is now in week 20, with no answer yet on who rules Iran, who controls the Strait of Hormuz, or where the nuclear program stands.

2. Khamenei is being buried, and the strikes are widening. The hard facts moved all day. Per the Polymarket list, Iran is reportedly burying Ayatollah Ali Khamenei today in Mashhad as the ceasefire unravels, and Prof G's guests noted top Iranian leaders were killed in the first day of the war. Iran reported 14 killed and 78 wounded in the overnight US strikes across five provinces. US Central Command says it hit about 90 targets on July 8, following 80 the day before, and named new sites including the Bushehr naval base, Chabahar port again, and a bridge on Iran's strategic corridor to China and Russia. The stated trigger was Iran attacking three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

3. The war is spilling into the neighbors. The interceptions tell you how wide this got. Per the Polymarket list, Jordan intercepted eight Iranian ballistic missiles fired at its territory, Kuwait shot down three ballistic missiles, a cruise missile, and ten drones at dawn with one injury and debris damage, and Bahrain's US forces headquarters appears to have taken two missile hits overnight with sirens nationwide. Per the Kalshi list, Iran is now formally accusing the US of war crimes. One detail on the American side, the Secret Service made Trump fly the older Air Force One out of Turkey when the fighting resumed and had the press lower the window blinds.

4. Maine flipped to the Democrats, and it flipped fast. The race that resolved this morning kept moving. Per the Kalshi list, Maine's Senate seat went from Republican 52 to Democrat 48 on Monday to Democrat 63 and Republican 37 today, a 15-point swing in the party's favor after Graham Platner's exit cleared the field. Nirav Shah has now officially entered the Democratic primary at about a 15 percent chance of the nomination. Looking to 2028, Citadel's Ken Griffin said he would back Marco Rubio over JD Vance, per Axios.

5. The market ignored all of it, and that silence is its own story. Stocks rose into the bombing. Per the Kalshi list, US equities added roughly $500 billion in half an hour, the Magnificent Seven sits at its cheapest versus the S&P 500 in over a decade even as the top ten names now make up 43 percent of the index, and traders are the most bullish on the dollar in more than ten years. The catch is under the surface. Rate-hike odds before year end climbed to 56 percent, the NY Fed survey shows one-year inflation expectations up another 3.7 percent, and Japan's 10-year government bond yield hit its highest level in 30 years. A market this calm about a widening war and a hotter inflation read is either right or asleep.

6. Around the world, quickly.

  • Ukraine. Per the Polymarket list, three Kremlin sources say Putin is rejecting peace talks and is likely to escalate as long as Ukraine keeps striking Russian territory.
  • Europe. Per the Kalshi list, the European Parliament passed the Chat Control proposal that allows private messages to be monitored, a major surveillance shift for the bloc.
  • The Gulf's long game. Per CaspianReport, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Oman are racing to build pipelines that bypass the Strait of Hormuz, but even at full capacity they cannot replace the volume that moves through it. The point is to survive a closure, not to defeat one.

item.title: Iran fired missiles at US bases across the Gulf, the US answered with a second round of strikes, and by 1am Trump said Iran wants a deal
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A night that started with rockets hitting American forces in Bahrain and Kuwait ended with the President floating negotiations, the sharpest whipsaw yet in a war that keeps being declared over. At home, the 2026 Senate map came into focus and Maine finally resolved. Here is what moved overnight.
item.content:encoded:
A night that started with rockets hitting American forces in Bahrain and Kuwait ended with the President floating negotiations, the sharpest whipsaw yet in a war that keeps being declared over. At home, the 2026 Senate map came into focus and Maine finally resolved. Here is what moved overnight.

The overnight news was war, and then, abruptly, maybe peace. Iran hit American bases across the Gulf, the US struck back harder, and within hours Trump was talking about a deal. Underneath the whiplash, the shape of the 2026 Senate election got clearer and a marquee race that looked like chaos suddenly had an answer. Here is the update.

1. Iran hit US bases, the US struck back, and then Trump floated a deal. The escalation was real before the reversal was. Per the Financial Markets list, Iran launched missiles at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, with two striking the US forces headquarters in Bahrain and Kuwait's army intercepting incoming fire. Per the Polymarket list, US Central Command answered with strikes on Chabahar, Bandar Abbas, and Qeshm Island, knocking out power in Chabahar, while Iran's parliament speaker warned the Strait of Hormuz only opens on Iranian terms. Then, per the Market list, Trump said around 1am that Iran had called and wanted a deal, and the whole risk-off move reversed. Whether that is a genuine off-ramp or a pause between rounds is the question the region wakes up to.

2. The 2026 Senate map came into focus, and it favors Republicans. With the midterms sixteen months out, the picture is hardening. Per Election Time's race-by-race map, the projection is Republicans holding the Senate 53 to 47, with the real battlegrounds in Texas, where Ken Paxton leads Democrat James Talarico by under two points, Ohio, where John Husted edges Sherrod Brown, and North Carolina, where Roy Cooper is up nearly eight over Michael Whatley in what the map calls the most likely flip. Georgia's Jon Ossoff, a 2028 hopeful, leads by six. The map that decides control runs through a handful of states, and right now it leans red.

3. Maine finally resolved after weeks of chaos. The messiest race got an answer overnight. Per the Kalshi list, Democrat Graham Platner officially suspended his campaign as his exit odds hit 99% on a video release, ending the sexual-misconduct saga that had whipsawed the market for a week. Former state senate president Troy Jackson immediately announced and is now the front-runner for the July 31 Democratic nomination at 47%, with incumbent Republican Susan Collins leading the overall race at 40% but Democrats favored to hold the seat at 64%, the highest since May. One trader reportedly cleared $173,769 on the Platner exit market alone.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Iraq and Syria. Per the Polymarket list, Iraq agreed to stop dollar flows to Iran-backed militias, and Trump rescinded Syria's state-sponsor-of-terrorism designation, two moves that reshuffle the region's alignments around the Iran fight.
  • South Korea. Per the Financial Markets list, the Kospi is now down 25% from the record it set just three weeks ago, a full bear market driven by the unwind in its two chip giants, even as the Bank of Korea signals a rate hike may be needed.
  • China. Per the Kalshi list, factory-gate inflation came in at its highest in about four years, a tentative sign the long deflation may be turning.
  • The AI jobs question. Per the Kalshi list, a Forbes read of the latest data notes that firms are now spending more on AI than they save from the employees it replaces, a warning that the productivity payoff still trails the bill.

item.title: Trump declared the Iran ceasefire dead, threatened to seize an oil island, and the war he ended in June is back on
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The US followed its overnight strike by hitting Bandar Abbas, Iran fired at Gulf bases, and the President floated taking Kharg Island as oil pushed past 80. The whole episode is accelerating a global arms race, and at home Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
The US followed its overnight strike by hitting Bandar Abbas, Iran fired at Gulf bases, and the President floated taking Kharg Island as oil pushed past 80. The whole episode is accelerating a global arms race, and at home Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning's strike was not the end of it. By evening the US had widened the campaign, Iran had answered, and the President was talking about seizing Iranian territory. The war Washington declared over in June is fully back, and its shockwaves are running through oil, defense budgets, and the 2028 race. Here is the update.

1. The US widened the Iran war and threatened to take an oil island. The escalation kept building all day. Per the Polymarket list, after the overnight strike on more than 80 targets, the US hit Bandar Abbas, and Trump declared the ceasefire and memorandum over, calling Iran's leaders scum, saying "we attacked Kharg Island last night" and that the US may take it over, and promising to hit them hard again tonight. Iran fired back, with Kuwait intercepting two ballistic missiles and thirteen drones and sirens sounding in Bahrain. Kharg Island handles a large share of Iran's oil exports, so a threat to seize it is a threat to the oil market itself, and that is why Brent pushed past $80.

2. The war is pouring fuel on a global arms race. The spending follows the fear. Per Prof G Markets, the NATO summit in Turkey produced more than $50B in new defense-industry deals plus $40B for counter-drone systems, and Trump's 2027 budget carries $1.5 trillion for defense, the largest ever. Defense-technology venture funding has already passed $19B in six months, beating all of last year. The detail that lands hardest is where the software companies now sit: Palantir's Maven targeting system, paired with Claude, was used in the Iran war, which puts the same names that sell you consumer AI squarely inside the kill chain.

3. Gavin Newsom's 2028 case is falling apart. The Democratic front-runner is fading fast. Per the All-In Podcast, Nate Silver lays out why: Newsom has dropped from roughly 25% to 15% in primary polls and from 33% to 22% on the betting markets, he was running on name recognition that is now meeting younger rivals like Georgia's Jon Ossoff, the party is trending toward fresher faces including Zohran Mamdani and AOC, and Newsom has tied himself to continuity with Biden and Harris, the exact strategy that already lost. It is a defensive posture for a man who was supposed to be the presumptive choice.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • South Korea. Per the Kalshi list, the Kospi has fallen into a bear market with roughly $968B wiped out in three weeks and a 5.6% crash today that triggered a trading halt, as its two chip giants, Samsung and SK Hynix, unwound.
  • United States, housing. Per Anthony Pompliano, a Dallas Fed paper found that a 1% rise in unauthorized workers lifted home prices about 2.2%, and the H-1B crackdown is now running that in reverse, with home prices in the fast-growing Texas suburbs of Collin County down almost 9% year over year.
  • Spain. Per the Polymarket list, Trump ordered a cutoff of all trade with Spain, calling it a wasted cause and a terrible NATO partner that does not pay.
  • Maine. Per the Kalshi list, the Senate race whipsawed again, with the party odds snapping back to Democrats at 61% and Republican Susan Collins leading the individual field at 43% as Graham Platner's exit odds cooled through the day.

item.title: The US bombed more than 80 targets in Iran overnight after Tehran hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz, and oil is the first thing paying for it
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A broken ceasefire and three attacked vessels drew a major American strike, sending crude up 5% and halting gas shipments through the Gulf. At home, the jobs picture keeps quietly rotting and the political map swung hard toward Democrats overnight. Here is what moved.
item.content:encoded:
A broken ceasefire and three attacked vessels drew a major American strike, sending crude up 5% and halting gas shipments through the Gulf. At home, the jobs picture keeps quietly rotting and the political map swung hard toward Democrats overnight. Here is what moved.

The overnight news was war. The US answered an attack on Gulf shipping with a large strike inside Iran, and the immediate cost is showing up in the oil price. Underneath it, the American labor market is sending a quieter warning and the 2028 political picture reshuffled overnight. Here is the update.

1. The US struck Iran after it attacked ships in Hormuz. The ceasefire is over. Per the Market list, the US hit more than 80 targets inside Iran overnight after Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, and crude jumped about 5% on the news. Per the Polymarket list, the Treasury revoked Iran's oil-export license, gas shipments through Hormuz have halted, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is set to visit Israel and Netanyahu today. This is the sharpest escalation of the conflict yet, and because roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through that strait, the market is treating it as a supply shock first and a diplomatic story second.

2. The American jobs picture is quietly deteriorating. The slow story matters as much as the loud one. Per Prof G Markets, the labor force participation rate fell, and the drop is concentrated in prime-age workers between 25 and 54, the cohort that cannot be explained away by boomer retirement. The share of the unemployed who have been out of work for six months or longer has now risen for eighteen straight months with no sign of slowing. The picture is not a collapse and not a recovery, it is a market where there are simply fewer opportunities, the kind of grinding weakness that does not make a headline until it suddenly does.

3. The political map swung toward Democrats overnight. The prediction markets moved hard. Per the Kalshi list, the odds that Maine Democrat Graham Platner drops out crashed from a 96% high yesterday to a 38% low, a wild reversal that keeps him in a race many had written off. At the same time the market for Democrats sweeping the presidency, House, and Senate hit an all-time high of 51%, the odds of a House flip reached 82%, and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez climbed to 18% for the 2028 nomination, within striking distance of Gavin Newsom. A single overnight turned a story of Democratic disarray into one of Democratic momentum.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • China. Per the Polymarket list, China will produce more than 100,000 humanoid robots this year and its central bank is extending its longest gold-buying streak since 2015, two moves that both read as preparing for a more self-reliant future.
  • France. Per the Kalshi list, Marine Le Pen has entered the 2027 race and is the favorite at 38% despite her legal troubles.
  • Argentina. Per the same list, Javier Milei sits at 57% for re-election, a vote of confidence in his shock therapy.
  • United Kingdom. Per the same list, Reform UK is a 96% favorite to win the Clacton by-election triggered by Nigel Farage's resignation.
  • South Korea. Per the Financial Markets list, roughly 183 trillion won, about $123B, was wiped from the Korean market in the semis selloff, even as SK Hynix's $28B US listing came in oversubscribed.

item.title: Iran's missiles in the Strait of Hormuz hit a Qatari gas tanker, and Trump remade a chunk of foreign policy in a single afternoon
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Gulf strike put energy shipping back in the crosshairs, the President lifted Turkey's sanctions and floated taking Greenland, and the Democratic Party's civil war got mapped out in real time. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
The Gulf strike put energy shipping back in the crosshairs, the President lifted Turkey's sanctions and floated taking Greenland, and the Democratic Party's civil war got mapped out in real time. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning left Iran as a headline risk. By evening it was a hit ship, a scrambled map of American foreign policy, and a Democratic Party openly at war with itself. Here is the update.

1. Iran's Hormuz strike hit a Qatari gas tanker, and that changes the stakes. The escalation got specific. Per the Polymarket list and the Crypto list, Iran fired at least two missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight and struck a Qatari LNG carrier, pulling liquefied natural gas into the same risk premium that already sits on oil. A missile that hits empty water is a warning. One that hits a Gulf ally's gas tanker is an event, and it puts the energy-price tail risk back on the table just as inflation expectations were already climbing.

2. Trump remade foreign policy in an afternoon. Three moves, one day. Per the Polymarket list, Trump lifted all US sanctions on Turkey and opened the door to selling it F-35s ahead of a bilateral with Erdogan in Ankara, and separately said Greenland "should be controlled by the United States." Taken together it is a sharp pivot toward transactional, territory-minded dealmaking, rewarding a NATO partner that had been frozen out while reviving a claim most people had written off as a first-term provocation.

3. The Democratic Party's civil war is now out in the open. Two threads pointed at the same fracture. Per the All-In Podcast, Nate Silver mapped the party into three warring camps, the progressive left of Zohran Mamdani, AOC, and Bernie Sanders, the free-market "abundance" liberals around Ezra Klein, and the "resistance" liberals who believe Biden was railroaded out and who form Gavin Newsom's base.

The Maine Senate race is where the fight turned bloody. Per the Polymarket list, Democrat Graham Platner is now better than 70% to drop out by tomorrow after Mamdani publicly called for him to quit and a new market opened on whether he gets criminally charged, though per the Kalshi list the drop-out odds actually cooled from 83% to 60% intraday. Either way the Democrats still hold the seat at 57%.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Syria. Per the Crypto list, a twin bombing in Damascus near the Four Seasons and Macron's hotel wounded 18, a jarring strike during a high-profile diplomatic visit.
  • France. Per the same list, Marine Le Pen has been cleared to run in 2027, back in the race while wearing an ankle monitor.
  • United Kingdom. Per the Crypto list, Nigel Farage resigned his seat as an MP, triggering a Clacton by-election.
  • United States, corporate. Microsoft cut its entire Xbox division, roughly 5,500 to 6,400 jobs, another reminder that the layoffs are landing in the parts of Big Tech that are not the AI story.
  • United States, markets. Per Prof G Markets, Dell added about a billion dollars of market value within hours after Trump told people on live television to "go buy a Dell," a stock his own disclosures show he bought nearly a million dollars of last year.

item.title: Iran put missiles back on the shipping lanes in the Strait of Hormuz, and Maine's Senate race fell apart overnight
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A fresh strike at Gulf shipping revived the oil-and-war premium, a Democratic front-runner collapsed as his own party pulled its endorsements, and six million kids are now signed up for the government's new investment accounts. Here is what moved overnight.
item.content:encoded:
A fresh strike at Gulf shipping revived the oil-and-war premium, a Democratic front-runner collapsed as his own party pulled its endorsements, and six million kids are now signed up for the government's new investment accounts. Here is what moved overnight.

Quiet morning at home, loud one abroad and in the markets. Iran reminded everyone the Gulf is still a tinderbox, a marquee Senate race imploded in a single day, and the government's plan to hand every child a brokerage account crossed its first big milestone. Here is the update.

1. Iran fired missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz again. The Gulf risk is back on the board. Per the Financial Markets list, Iran reportedly fired at least two missiles at ships in the Strait of Hormuz overnight, the chokepoint through which a large share of the world's oil and jet fuel moves. Even a limited strike puts a premium back into crude and shipping, and it lands at a moment when gold is negative on the year and traders had grown comfortable that the summer would stay calm. The market reaction will tell you whether this reads as a one-off or the start of another escalation cycle.

2. Maine's Senate race came apart in a single day. Watch how fast a front-runner can fall. Per the Kalshi list, the odds that Democrat Graham Platner drops out of the Maine Senate race ramped through the day from 16% to 85% before settling near 76%, after Elizabeth Warren, Ro Khanna, and Ruben Gallego all pulled their endorsements over allegations Platner calls categorically false. The scramble reset the whole contest, with Troy Jackson now the favorite for the Democratic nomination at 62% and incumbent Republican Susan Collins and Jackson running close in the head-to-head. A race that looked settled at breakfast was a different race by dinner.

3. Six million kids are signed up for Trump Accounts, and how you use one matters. The government's child-investing program cleared a real milestone. Per the Polymarket list, Robinhood says six million children have now signed up for Trump Accounts, and Gwynne Shotwell is donating SpaceX shares into more than two million of them. The accounts drop a one-time $1,000 into an S&P 500 fund per eligible child.

The mechanics reward planning. Per BowTied Bull, a Trump Account takes up to $5,000 a year and behaves like an IRA the child controls at 18, and paired with a 529 that can roll about $35,000 into a Roth and a taxable custodial account, a disciplined $19,000 a year across the three can compound past $600,000 by adulthood. The same piece flags the obvious risk of handing an 18-year-old a seven-figure balance with no strings, and makes the case for trusts with age and milestone triggers instead.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • South Korea. Per the Kalshi list, the Kospi fell 5.7% and authorities halted program selling after Samsung's revenue miss, a reminder of how much of that index rides on two chipmakers.
  • Texas. Per the Polymarket list, Toyota is putting $3.6B into a Texas plant and moving some Tacoma production out of Mexico, roughly 2,000 jobs, the latest concrete reshoring receipt.
  • United States. Per the same list, Trump touted Walmart cutting prices, including about 15% off ground beef, as evidence the inflation fight is turning.
  • China. Per the Stocks list, Alibaba is barring its staff from using Anthropic's Claude Code by July 10 amid a dispute over China-detection code, another brick in the US-China tech wall.
  • Washington. Per the Kalshi list, a Treasury internal report is now flagging AI-bubble risk, a notable tell when the official machinery starts naming the thing out loud.

item.title: Washington started depositing 1,000 dollars into every newborn's brokerage account, and billionaires lined up to add to the pot
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Trump Accounts went live over the holiday with rare bipartisan backing and billions in private pledges, Russia hit Kyiv the day after a call with Trump, and the AI layoffs arrived with a very careful denial. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
Trump Accounts went live over the holiday with rare bipartisan backing and billions in private pledges, Russia hit Kyiv the day after a call with Trump, and the AI layoffs arrived with a very careful denial. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning was quiet, the evening filled in the edges. A new government program put investment accounts in the hands of every American child, the war in Ukraine escalated right after a leaders' call, and a fresh round of tech layoffs came wrapped in a careful message about who is really being replaced. Here is the update.

1. The government is now seeding a brokerage account for every newborn, and the money is bipartisan. This is the domestic story of the weekend. Per Anthony Pompliano, Trump Accounts went live on July 4, a tax-deferred custodial account that drops a one-time $1,000 government deposit into an S&P 500 fund for every US child born between 2025 and 2028, with families able to add up to $5,000 a year and the balance locked until age 18 for college, a business, or a home.

The private money piled in on top. Michael and Susan Dell pledged $6.25B to seed accounts for 25 million children, Gwynne Shotwell and her husband committed 2 million SpaceX shares worth more than $300M, and JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Coinbase each rolled out programs to match another $1,000 per employee child. Per the People-to-follow list, Trump personally rang in the launch for 1.4 million accounts. The rare part is the politics: this drew support across the aisle, a genuine attempt to hand the next generation a stake in the market rather than an argument about it.

2. Russia hit Kyiv the day after a Putin-Trump call, and Ukraine struck deep into Russia in return. The war took a harder turn. Per the Polymarket list, Russia killed at least eight people in a barrage on Kyiv just after a July 4 phone call between Putin and Trump, the kind of timing that reads as a message rather than a coincidence. Ukraine answered with its deepest strike of the war, hitting an oil refinery in Omsk some 2,500 kilometers inside Russia, extending its campaign against the fuel that moves the Russian military. A call meant to lower the temperature was followed within a day by an escalation on both ends.

3. The AI layoffs arrived with a careful denial, and the squeeze underneath it is real. The job cuts came, and so did the messaging around them. Per the People-to-follow list, Microsoft is cutting about 4,800 jobs, roughly 2.1% of its workforce, and its HR chief Amy Coleman went out of her way to say those workers are not being replaced by AI, while Porsche announced 4,000 cuts in Germany. The denial is doing a lot of work in a week when every headline is about automation.

Underneath the layoffs is a slower grind. Per ClearValue Tax, wages are up 23% since 2021 while prices are up 28%, property insurance has climbed 75% in five years, housing now eats more than 40% of the typical income, and credit card debt has hit a record $1.25T with delinquencies at their highest since 2008. The Trump Accounts are a bet on the next generation precisely because this generation cannot get ahead.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • China. Per the Polymarket list, Beijing test-fired a ballistic missile from a nuclear submarine into the Pacific, a rare and deliberate show of reach.
  • China, again. Per the same list, regulators forced ByteDance and Alibaba to pull AI-companion features from their apps, a sharp turn against the consumer AI its own firms are building.
  • Saudi Arabia. Per the same list, Riyadh cut its August Arab Light price to Asia by $11 a barrel, the largest such cut in more than 26 years, a signal it is fighting for market share as OPEC+ lifts output.
  • United States. Per a Prof G Markets clip, Scott Galloway used the fight over rolling back 14th Amendment birthright citizenship to needle Stephen Miller and Trump, noting that under the proposed change he, the son of immigrants, would not be a citizen, and joking he would like his decade of taxes back.

item.title: A joint rebel-jihadist offensive hit Mali's army and its Russian mercenaries on the Fourth of July, and America's 2028 race is already taking shape a year early
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Sahel militants coordinated an attack that killed around 20 in Sevare, JD Vance pulled clear of the Republican field, and Britain's politics flipped as Labour edged back ahead of Reform. Here is what moved over the holiday.
item.content:encoded:
Sahel militants coordinated an attack that killed around 20 in Sevare, JD Vance pulled clear of the Republican field, and Britain's politics flipped as Labour edged back ahead of Reform. Here is what moved over the holiday.

The holiday weekend was quiet at home and busy at the edges. A coordinated offensive in the Sahel put Russia's African footprint back in the headlines, the American 2028 field started to harden a full year before anyone votes, and Britain's two-party fight swung again. Here is what happened.

1. Militants hit Mali's army and its Russian mercenaries in a coordinated Fourth of July offensive. The Sahel escalation is the real news of the weekend. Per the Polymarket list, a separatist force and the al-Qaeda-linked JNIM launched a joint offensive on July 4 against Mali's ruling junta and the Africa Corps mercenaries who prop it up, with around 20 people reported killed in the central town of Sevare. The detail that matters is the coordination. A Tuareg separatist movement and a jihadist group are ideological rivals, and when they attack the same target together it points at a junta losing control of its own center, not just its periphery. It also drags Moscow in, because the Africa Corps presence is the thing keeping this government standing.

2. The 2028 American field is already hardening, and Vance is pulling clear. A year out, the prediction markets are starting to converge. Per the Kalshi list, JD Vance now sits at 41% to be the 2028 Republican nominee, up from 32% a month ago, the kind of move that signals a field consolidating around a front-runner rather than splintering. The down-ballot action is in Michigan, where the same market has the Democratic primary reshuffling hard: Abdul El-Sayed slipped from 90% to 80%, Haley Stevens doubled from 11% to 22%, and the odds of Republicans taking the Senate seat at all climbed from 19% to 30%. Early markets are noisy, but a 10-point swing in a month is a real shift in who the serious money thinks wins.

3. Britain's politics flipped back, with Labour edging ahead of Reform. The UK story is a reversal. Per the Kalshi list, Labour has climbed to 37% in the next-general-election market and overtaken Nigel Farage's Reform at 33%, unwinding the Reform lead that had defined British polling for months. It is a market read rather than a ballot, so treat it as sentiment, but the direction is the point. The insurgent-right surge that looked inevitable through the spring has stalled, and the governing party is clawing back the lead it was supposed to have lost for good.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Hungary. Per the Polymarket list, a bill to remove President Tamas Sulyok, an Orban ally, has the market at 68% for an ouster this month, a rare and serious move against the ruling bloc.
  • Taiwan. Per the same list, Taipei is flagging a jump in Chinese naval activity around the island alongside joint China-Russia drills, the kind of tempo increase that gets watched for a pattern rather than a one-off.
  • Ukraine. Per the Financial Markets list, Ukrainian long-range strikes have now disrupted an estimated 15 to 30% of Russia's refining capacity, a slow attritional campaign aimed at the fuel behind the front.
  • Oil. Per the same list, Saudi Arabia pushed crude exports to a record near 5 million barrels a day as OPEC+ lifts output, and prices fell on the extra supply.
  • South Korea. Per the Polymarket list, Seoul is beginning 24-hour trading in the won, a structural step toward treating its currency like a global one.
  • United States. Per the Financial Markets list, the first joint New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq bell-ringing from the Oval Office is set for Monday morning to mark the launch of Trump Accounts.

item.title: The unemployment rate says the labor market is fine, and almost every other number this week says it is quietly coming apart
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: AI got named in 101,743 job cuts, the household survey shows well over a million jobs gone this year, and workers aged 22 to 25 are down 13% since the models got good. This was the week the AI jobs squeeze stopped being a forecast.
item.content:encoded:
AI got named in 101,743 job cuts, the household survey shows well over a million jobs gone this year, and workers aged 22 to 25 are down 13% since the models got good. This was the week the AI jobs squeeze stopped being a forecast.

The loudest number in economics is the one hiding the story. The headline unemployment rate sat near 4.2% all week and the recession bet kept losing, and underneath both, four separate editions surfaced the same quiet crack: artificial intelligence is starting to reprice who is employable, it is landing hardest on the young and the entry-level, and the topline data is built to miss it. This is the story that will define the politics of the next two years more than any monthly jobs print, so it is worth pulling the week's threads into one place.

What actually happened this week. The evidence arrived in four waves, each from a different source, each pointing the same direction.

  • AI became the single biggest named reason for layoffs. The Challenger layoff data walked through on ClearValue Tax put AI behind 101,743 job cuts year to date, about 23% of all cuts and 31% of June's alone, up from low single digits historically. Tech as a sector accounts for roughly a third of all cuts this year, 139,156 and up 83%. That is a category that essentially did not exist as a layoff reason two years ago.
  • The household survey says the jobs are actually gone. Per both the Polymarket and Financial Markets feeds, the household survey shows the US has shed more than 1.7 million jobs so far this year, pushing employment to its lowest since December 2024, even as the odds of a recession this year stayed near record lows. By Saturday the Polymarket list added that more than 1.8 million Americans are now averaging at least 27 weeks of unemployment, the long-term-jobless measure that tends to move last and matter most.
  • The damage is concentrated on the young. The clearest signal came from a Stanford and ADP study relayed on the Crypto list: workers aged 22 to 25 in AI-exposed jobs are down 13% since late 2022, recent-graduate unemployment sits at 5.6% against a 4.3% national rate, and 42.5% of graduates are underemployed. The machine is eating the bottom rung of the ladder first, the exact jobs a new worker used to climb through.
  • Fear caught up to the data. By the weekend the Polymarket list reported half of all Americans now say they fear AI could cost them or a family member a job, and in a detail that tells you where the displacement runs, AI hiring in India's IT sector is rising even as overall recruitment there falls. The tool is not destroying work evenly. It is moving it.

The counterweight, because it is real. The honest version of this story includes the other side. The same Stanford and ADP data shows that firms adopting AI early are still adding people, with Ramp data putting AI-adopter headcount up 10.2% and entry-level roles up 12% at those firms. So the picture is not "AI destroys jobs," it is a divergence: the companies that wield the tool are hiring, and the workers and firms that do not are being hollowed out. That gap, adopters pulling ahead while everyone else sheds, is the actual shape of what is happening, and it is a more uncomfortable story than a simple jobs apocalypse because it has winners standing right next to the losers.

The frame the week kept reaching for. Two of the sharpest cultural takes of the week were really about this same divide. David Friedberg's makers-versus-takers immigration test argued that the only question that matters is whether a person comes to produce or to consume, and his faith that any productive person grows the economy runs headfirst into a labor market that is quietly deleting the entry-level rungs where productivity used to start. Scott Galloway's creator-economy read from Cannes, that brand spend is up 24% while the money flows to nano and micro creators and "the most takes the most," is the same winner-take-more dynamic in a different industry. The through-line across immigration, media, and the jobs data is a single unsettling idea: the economy is bifurcating into those who direct the tools and those the tools direct around.

What to watch next. Three things will tell you whether this is a soft patch or a structural break. First, watch the divergence between the establishment survey and the household survey. The headline rate is smoothing over a household-level contraction, and when those two finally reconcile, the reconciliation is the news. Second, watch the graduate class of 2026 hit the market this summer, because a 5.6% recent-grad unemployment rate and 42.5% underemployment is the leading edge, and it either eases as adopters keep hiring or it hardens into a lost cohort. Third, watch the politics. A labor market that looks fine on paper while half the country fears for its job is a combustible gap, and it is the kind of gap that gets legislated, taxed, or campaigned on well before the official statistics admit there is a problem. The number to distrust for the next two years is the unemployment rate. The numbers to trust are the ones underneath it.

item.title: Khamenei's own son skipped his funeral, and China put a number on the robots taking the jobs: 700,000, with 120 schools to retrain the survivors
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A missing heir hinted at a succession fight in Tehran, JD.com named the workforce it will automate away, and Europe spent the weekend discovering its money and its rules do not reach as far as it thought. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
A missing heir hinted at a succession fight in Tehran, JD.com named the workforce it will automate away, and Europe spent the weekend discovering its money and its rules do not reach as far as it thought. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning edition left Iran in a mourning pause and the robots as a projection. By evening both had a sharper edge: a succession signal from a funeral, and a company naming the exact number of jobs it will erase. Underneath, Europe had a quietly bad weekend on two fronts at once. Here is the update.

1. The missing mourner in Tehran is the story. Per the Polymarket list, Mojtaba Khamenei, long treated as a possible successor to his father, was reportedly absent from Ali Khamenei's funeral while the rest of Iran's top officials attended. In a system where presence at the funeral is a claim on power, an absence like that reads as friction inside the succession rather than around it. It advances this morning's thread, the US-granted mourning pause and the July 11 talks in Pakistan, by pointing at the question those talks will actually turn on: who Washington will be negotiating with a year from now. In a related move, per the same list, France is sending its carrier the Charles de Gaulle home after roughly two months stationed near the Strait of Hormuz, a quiet de-escalation signal from Europe.

2. China put a hard number on automation, and a retraining plan under it. Per a Nikkei-sourced report, JD.com CEO Liu Qiangdong says robots will replace about 700,000 of the company's delivery workers, and JD has signed with roughly 120 schools to retrain those workers into robot repair and maintenance roles. Beijing has reportedly started tracking AI's hit to employment as a national priority. The comparison that frames it is Amazon avoiding around 600,000 future hires through automation, and the read-through names for who follows next are DoorDash, Uber, and MercadoLibre. This is the concrete escalation of the morning's jobs story: not a warning about displacement, but a headcount, a timeline, and a plan for what happens to the people.

3. Europe found the limits of its money and its rules in the same weekend. Two stories rhymed. On rules, per the Milk Road letter, the MiCA transition window shut on July 1, and roughly 80% of about 1,200 EU crypto firms lack a compliant license and must now wind down, migrate, or leave, even as Brussels fast-tracks a digital euro. On money, per the Polymarket list, France drew 15.1 billion euros from the EU's SAFE defense fund against the 16.2 billion it requested after Franco-British missile projects were ruled ineligible under a 65% EU-origin rule, and Germany's cabinet is set to approve a budget with more than 203 billion euros in fresh borrowing while 20,000 protesters gathered at the AfD's annual conference. A continent trying to build sovereignty in defense and finance kept bumping into its own rulebook.

Around the world, quickly.

  • France. Per the Polymarket list, a court ordered luxury magnate Bernard Arnault to pay $25.73M in back taxes.
  • Hungary. Per the same list, lawmakers filed a bill seeking to oust President Tamas Sulyok, an Orban ally, a rare move against the ruling bloc.
  • Arabian Sea. The US Navy suspended its search for a sailor missing since July 1 after an MH-60S helicopter went down near the carrier George H.W. Bush.
  • Ukraine. Kyiv says Russia destroyed or damaged more than 200 railway locomotives across 2026, a slow attritional strike on the logistics network behind the front.
  • United States, 2028. Per the Kalshi list, Gavin Newsom leads the Democratic nomination market at 21%, down from a 37% high in November, and John James sits at 84% to win Michigan's GOP gubernatorial primary.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the memory price hikes, the neocloud selloff, the SK Hynix IPO, and the Bitcoin outflow story all went there as market material. Cross-routed to AI & Tools: China's LongCat-2.0 open model, Alibaba banning Claude Code internally, and the two new Polymarket markets on the US pulling access to major AI models went there as the model-and-policy story. Dropped: a Vox Day fiction serial (no news), and a thin get-rich listicle with no argument.

item.title: Trump handed Iran a week off for Khamenei's funeral, and the robots came for 700,000 jobs in a single announcement
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan and Netanyahu heads to the White House, Ukrainian drones reached 850 kilometers inside Russia, and JD.com said it will replace 700,000 delivery workers with machines. The AI jobs story stopped being a projection.
item.content:encoded:
US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan and Netanyahu heads to the White House, Ukrainian drones reached 850 kilometers inside Russia, and JD.com said it will replace 700,000 delivery workers with machines. The AI jobs story stopped being a projection.

Independence Day weekend gave the feed a split screen: a careful pause in one war and fresh strikes deep inside another, a president clearing 702 rules while his rival warned about democracy, and the clearest sign yet that automation is now a headcount decision, not a thought experiment. Here is what actually moved.

1. The US gave Iran a week off, and the calendar now has real dates on it. Per the Polymarket list, Trump revealed the US granted Iran a pause for the funeral of the late Ayatollah Khamenei, with no shooting until the funeral events conclude, and formal US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan covering sanctions, frozen Iranian assets, and the nuclear file. Alongside it, Netanyahu is reportedly headed to the White House as early as next week. This is the diplomacy that yesterday's Strait of Hormuz brinkmanship was building toward, and the fact that both sides agreed to a mourning pause is itself the signal that neither wants the shooting war right now.

2. Ukraine reached 850 kilometers into Russia while the Kremlin said nothing has changed. Per the Polymarket list, Ukrainian drones struck Russian oil sites more than 850 kilometers inside Russia on Saturday and destroyed a MiG-29 at the Belbek airbase in Crimea. The Kremlin's Dmitry Peskov reaffirmed the war continues and said Zelensky is welcome to come to Moscow, while Dmitry Medvedev framed the Strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb as Iran's non-nuclear weapons. The reach of those strikes is the story: the war's front line is now wherever a cheap drone can fly, and Russian oil infrastructure hundreds of miles from Ukraine is inside it.

3. The AI jobs story went from projection to headcount. Per the Market list, JD.com CEO Liu Qiangdong warned that robots will replace 700,000 of the company's delivery workers, and the company has signed with about 120 schools to retrain those workers into robot-repair roles, with Beijing now tracking the AI jobs hit as a national priority. It echoes the earlier Amazon figure of 600,000 future hires avoided, and per the Financial Markets list, BMW is already deploying humanoid robots at its South Carolina factory. This is the concrete version of the labor-market crack the weekend memo traced: not a statistic about displacement, but a company naming the number of jobs and the schools it will send those people to instead.

4. Trump moved to cut 702 federal regulations. Per the Kalshi list, the administration announced plans to eliminate 702 federal regulations. On the same July 4 tape, Bill Clinton used a holiday statement to warn that America faces serious threats to its democracy, and Zelensky said he had a very good call with Trump. The deregulation push is the substantive policy move under the holiday noise, and it is the one to track for what actually gets struck.

5. A heat wave is stress-testing the grid, and the power price is the tell. Per the People-to-follow list, extreme heat is pushing the central and eastern US toward 105°F, and Thursday's wholesale power spot prices jumped hard: New England up 243%, New York City up 101%, the Midwest up 55%, and the Mid-Atlantic up 45.6%, with PJM placed under a federal alert to cut usage. The AI-and-cooling load and the summer heat are hitting the same grid at the same time, and the wholesale price is where that collision shows up first. The investable side of this, the power and grid names, sits in Investing.

Around the world, quickly.

  • Venezuela. Per the Polymarket list, the death toll from the June 24 earthquakes rose to 2,954 dead and 16,592 injured, per Parliament President Jorge Rodríguez.
  • Germany. AfD leader Alice Weidel claimed most migrants will return home once they stop receiving money from the German state, sharpening the same benefits-versus-work line that ran through the weekend's immigration debate.
  • Australia. New South Wales confirmed its first human H5N1 bird-flu case, a marker worth tracking rather than alarm.
  • Indonesia. Per the Financial Markets list, the country jailed entrepreneur Nadiem Makarim, the Gojek founder and former education minister, for roughly a decade.
  • Paul Pelosi. Per the Polymarket list, he reportedly faces a hit-and-run charge after allegedly striking a parked car and driving away.
  • America 250, the messy version. Severe storms cleared the National Mall before Trump's speech, which slid to 11pm, and the Brooklyn Bridge briefly caught fire during New York's fireworks. The anniversary went off, just not on script.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: Kevin Warsh's argument that AI is deflationary, as a macro and rate story. Cross-routed to Investing: the record $15B in Chinese chip exports to Hong Kong. Dropped: the America250 "250 years of predictions" series, the Joey Chestnut hot-dog title, the Ben Franklin look-alike contest, and the World Cup announcer and tailgate novelty markets, all holiday filler. Dropped: the Elon and Tesla July 4 promo quote-cards, no news.

item.title: Friedberg drew an immigration line by asking one question, and the AI job scare put numbers behind why it lands
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The makers-versus-takers test, 1.8 million Americans stuck in long-term unemployment as half the country fears AI takes the next job, and a Reuters tally that says the Trump family's crypto ventures are now worth $2.3 billion even as the tokens crater.
item.content:encoded:
The makers-versus-takers test, 1.8 million Americans stuck in long-term unemployment as half the country fears AI takes the next job, and a Reuters tally that says the Trump family's crypto ventures are now worth $2.3 billion even as the tokens crater.

Independence Day gave the feed a theme it did not plan: who gets to be an American, and what work is still worth doing once the machines want the same jobs. The immigration argument got a clean test, the labor data got grim, and the president's family got a $2.3 billion crypto scorecard. Below that, a quieter world kept moving.

1. Friedberg reduced the whole immigration fight to one question, and it is a good one. On the All-In podcast, David Friedberg made his immigration test not about culture or country of origin but about a single motive: are you coming to work or coming for benefits. His makers-versus-takers frame would deny entry to anyone whose primary aim is social support and grant it to anyone whose aim is to produce, earn, and advance their family. His deeper claim is the one people resist, that immigration is not a zero-sum fight over a fixed pile of jobs, because anyone who creates more value than they consume grows the economy and creates more jobs for everyone. Agree or not, it is the rare framing that gives both sides a rule instead of a slogan.

2. The AI job scare stopped being a vibe and started being a number. Friedberg's faith in work meets a labor market that is quietly hollowing. Per the Polymarket list, the count of Americans unemployed for at least 27 weeks has averaged above 1.8 million this year, half of Americans now say they fear AI could cost them or a family member a job, and in a tell about where the displacement runs, AI hiring in India's IT sector is rising even as overall recruitment there declines. The machines are not taking every job, they are taking the entry rungs and the routine middle, exactly the ladder Friedberg's makers climb. One more sign of the new normal from the same list: voters are reportedly turning to AI chatbots for help before casting their ballots, so the tool reshaping the labor market is now in the voting booth too.

3. The Trump family's crypto empire is now a $2.3 billion number, and the tokens are down anyway. Per Trader Mayne relaying Reuters, the Trump family's crypto ventures are collectively valued near $2.3 billion, even though the flagship assets have cratered: the TRUMP memecoin has generated roughly $635 million in fees while falling about 96% from its peak, and World Liberty Financial's WLFI token is down around 70% despite paper values in the hundreds of millions. The story is the gap. The ventures print fees and paper wealth for the family while retail holders eat the drawdown, the conflict-of-interest engine in one chart. It pairs with the fresh Trump Accounts wrinkle on the Polymarket list, where the Treasury will now reportedly accept donations of publicly traded stock to fund the new children's accounts.

4. Galloway put a price on the creator economy, and the money is leaving the middle. From Cannes Lions, Scott Galloway's read on the creator economy is that brand spending on creators is up about 24% year over year, with roughly half of it now flowing to nano and micro creators rather than the megastars. His blunt law of the attention economy is that the most takes the most, a winner-take-more dynamic that mirrors the same top-heavy pull reshaping labor and markets everywhere else in today's feed.

Around the world, quickly.

  • Iran. Per the Polymarket list, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's funeral procession will travel through five cities across Iran and Iraq, a staged succession moment for a state in transition.
  • Iran and China, at the strait. The same list reports Iran declaring that China will get special considerations in any new Strait of Hormuz fee arrangement, a sign the emerging chokepoint tolls will be political, not neutral.
  • Taiwan. China's Coast Guard deployed a new patrol task group east of Taiwan, per the Polymarket list, pushing its presence past the island's far side.
  • Moldova. The prime minister resigned, triggering the collapse of the government, another wobble on Europe's eastern edge.
  • Germany. Chancellor Friedrich Merz said Germany has no reason to shy away from its military spending push, while Berlin is reportedly weighing an end to its one-year crypto tax exemption under the 2027 budget.
  • Drones as an industry. German startup Quantum Systems raised $1.2 billion at an $8 billion valuation, per the Polymarket list, as Russia's largest jobs site advertises for drone operators to defend Moscow. The war economy is now a hiring market.
  • Canada. Ottawa unveiled plans for an oil pipeline to Asia in a bid to become an energy superpower and cut its dependence on the US.
  • Japan. Amid record immigration, Japan is hiking residence permit fees, including a reported 1,900% jump for permanent residency applications, the exact taker-screening instinct Friedberg was arguing about, applied with a price tag.

Filtered. Gold overtaking US government bonds as the world's top reserve asset appeared again today, but we covered it in the AM edition, so it stays noted rather than repeated. The Kalshi America250 novelty markets and the World Cup and hot-dog-contest prediction markets are holiday filler with no news value. A new Polymarket market asking whether the US government will remove public access to another Anthropic AI model in 2026 is a market to watch but not yet an event. The Trump-family crypto valuation is a politics story here, the memory and Bitcoin and neocloud material went to Investing, and the Fable 5 and agent-tooling items went to AI & Tools.

item.title: On the Fourth of July, the Supreme Court hands Trump a defeat on citizenship while his disclosure shows how much richer the office made him
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Court reportedly struck the birthright-citizenship order, a new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B with 327 stock buys the day before he paused tariffs, and the data says the AI squeeze is landing hardest on people under 25.
item.content:encoded:
The Court reportedly struck the birthright-citizenship order, a new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B with 327 stock buys the day before he paused tariffs, and the data says the AI squeeze is landing hardest on people under 25.

The holiday is quiet but two American stories underneath it are not. The Supreme Court dealt the White House a loss on one of its signature orders, and a fresh financial disclosure shows just how well the presidency has paid. Then the labor data keeps saying the same uncomfortable thing about who AI is actually displacing.

1. The Supreme Court reportedly struck down Trump's birthright-citizenship order. Per the All-In show notes, the Court ruled against the executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship, leaving the Fourteenth Amendment's guarantee in place. This is the show's framing rather than a court transcript, so treat the reasoning as still to be read in the opinion itself. The politics are clean regardless of the legal detail. A conservative-majority Court drawing a line on one of the administration's defining orders is the kind of check that reshapes what the next two years of executive action can attempt.

2. Trump's own disclosure shows how much the office is worth. A new filing puts the president's portfolio near $3.8B, including more than $1.3B in crypto-related income, per the People-to-follow list. The sharper detail sits in the Stocks list: Kalshi and NBC reporting flags that Trump-linked investment accounts made more than 300 stock purchases the day before he paused his tariffs, a sequence critics frame as pocketing roughly $1.4B on the reversal.

The timing lands against a new policy launching the same weekend. Per the Crypto list, "Trump Accounts" formally launch on July 4, running about a million signups a month, with the Treasury set to accept donations of publicly traded stock into them. Whatever the merits, the optics of a president disclosing billions while launching a stock-funded savings program on Independence Day will define the week's coverage.

3. The AI jobs squeeze is landing on the young, and the numbers are getting hard to wave off. The clearest signal in the Crypto list is generational. A Stanford and ADP study finds workers aged 22 to 25 in AI-exposed jobs are down 13% since late 2022, recent-graduate unemployment sits at 5.6% against a 4.3% national rate, and 42.5% of graduates are underemployed. The counterweight is that firms adopting AI early are still adding people, with Ramp data showing AI-adopter headcount up 10.2% and entry-level roles up 12%.

Read together, the story is not "AI took the jobs" but "AI is reshaping who gets hired first." The bottom rung of the ladder is where the pressure shows up earliest, and that is a political problem long before it is an economic one.

Around the world, quickly.

  • California and Colorado, two Democratic story lines. Per the All-In show notes, Governor Newsom is touting a balanced California budget that critics say papers over real fiscal strain, while a democratic-socialist candidate, Melat Kiros, reportedly pulled a primary upset in Colorado. Both feed the 2028 jockeying already showing up in prediction markets.
  • Argentina. Kalshi bettors put Javier Milei's standing near 58% per the Kalshi list, keeping his reform experiment the one to watch in Latin America.
  • The wealth-tax line keeps moving down. Per the Financial Markets list, Representative Ro Khanna argued a wealth tax "must not stop at billionaires" and should reach fortunes as low as $50M, a marker for where the Democratic left wants the debate.
  • Gold quietly became the world's largest reserve asset. Central-bank buying pushed gold past US Treasuries as the top reserve holding, per the Kalshi list, the same week US federal debt topped 100% of GDP for the first time since 1946. Lyn Alden's read on the Stocks list is that the deficit now exceeds all private bank lending combined.
  • Washington cancelled its July 4 parade for extreme heat. A small but telling detail from the Financial Markets list as much of the country bakes.
  • Prediction markets are becoming an asset class, and the regulators noticed. Bernstein projects a $1T industry by 2030, per the Financial Markets list, even as Europe's ESMA warns event contracts could fall under its 2018 binary-options retail ban.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Palantir and Nvidia open-model deal and Alex Karp's on-air combativeness came through the same All-In notes but belong to AI & Tools. The Trump portfolio's crypto-income line and the tariff-timed trades also carry a markets angle covered in Investing. The DeepSeek and Alibaba distillation fight went to AI & Tools. Tucker Carlson teasing an unspecified "major political plan" is a tease until there is a plan, so it waits.

item.title: The Strait of Hormuz is back as the world's most dangerous chokepoint
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Iran is threatening ships again as Brent slips under $72, Washington quietly warned Poland that Russia may stage a provocation, and over the holiday a lab in Minnesota built the first synthetic cell from scratch.
item.content:encoded:
Iran is threatening ships again as Brent slips under $72, Washington quietly warned Poland that Russia may stage a provocation, and over the holiday a lab in Minnesota built the first synthetic cell from scratch.

Two stories run under the holiday quiet, and both deserve more than the thin headlines gave them. The oil chokepoint that defined this year's war risk is live again, and the labor market is softening in a way the cheerful top-line numbers hide. Then, almost unnoticed, science had one of its biggest weeks in years.

1. Iran is threatening ships in the Strait of Hormuz again, and the oil market is starting to price it. The Financial Markets list has Iran warning of a "forceful response" against ships using unapproved routes through the Strait, the channel that carries a fifth of the world's oil. Brent crude (the international oil benchmark) slipped under $72 a barrel. The measured read comes from analyst Anas Alhajji: Iran cannot actually close the Strait, but it can make it expensive and slow, and shipping data already shows crossings down about 10% day over day. A new Polymarket contract now takes bets on whether traffic returns to normal by the end of August.

The escalation is not only in the Gulf. Washington reportedly warned Poland that Russia may stage a limited provocation against NATO in the coming months, a missile strike on infrastructure or a small cross-border incursion, calibrated to pressure allies over Ukraine without triggering a full war. Layer that over Ayatollah Khamenei's funeral drawing millions into Tehran today, and the geopolitical tail that markets spent the spring fearing is back on the table.

2. The labor market is cracking quietly, even as the recession bet keeps losing. Here is the disconnect worth holding onto. The household survey shows the US has lost 1.7 million jobs so far this year, dropping employment to its lowest level since December 2024, per both the Polymarket and Financial Markets feeds. Yet the odds of a US recession this year still sit near record lows.

Both things can be true at once, and that is the point. Lyn Alden's framing captures it: this is a two-speed economy, where older Americans on fixed income and defense contractors do fine while a lot of working people quietly do not. When the jobs data and the recession odds point in opposite directions, the jobs data is the one you can count.

3. Over the holiday, science had a genuinely historic week. This is the part that got buried under the market noise, and it should not have. From Packy McCormick's roundup, three real firsts landed at once.

  • A synthetic cell, built from non-living chemicals. A University of Minnesota team led by Kate Adamala assembled the first synthetic cell from scratch, one that can feed, grow, and reproduce. It is the biology equivalent of the Wright Flyer, crude and enormous at the same time.
  • Human eggs, grown from stem cells. Conception Bio, out of Berkeley, produced the first early human eggs starting from blood cells reprogrammed into an ovary. The implications for fertility are obvious and large.
  • A machine that reads words off your brain. Meta's non-invasive decoder hit 61% word accuracy, up from 8% for previous methods, translating brain activity into text without surgery.

And the nuclear industry hit a cluster of reactor "criticality" milestones (the point a reactor sustains its own chain reaction) around July 4, with Aalo Atomics, Radiant, and Deployable Energy all reaching or loading fuel. The AI-power crunch is pulling nuclear forward faster than almost anyone forecast a year ago.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Europe is turning on Palantir. Per the Financial Markets list, France, Germany, and Spain have instructed state-linked companies to avoid Palantir, and the UK's NHS is reviewing a £330M contract. The pushback is part politics and part a real fight over who controls government data.
  • The US-China AI wall got taller. Alibaba banned its own employees from using Claude Code, and Anthropic moved to close the loopholes that let Chinese firms reach its models, per Kalshi. The decoupling is now happening tool by tool, not just chip by chip.
  • Republicans firmed up their Senate edge. The GOP is now around 60% to hold the Senate on the prediction markets, a recent high. Slow drift in a party-control market usually means more than a single poll.
  • Trump says he "didn't know" he made $1.4B from crypto. Also on Kalshi, a disclosure of the president's crypto gains that will not be the last word on it.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The memory-stock fight, Bitcoin's contested bottom, the Meta and Micron capex battle, and Tom Lee's market call are all in Investing. The Fable 5 relaunch, the Anthropic profitability report, and the Etched inference chip are in AI & Tools. Nothing on the news side was dropped as noise today.

item.title: The government is trying to buy into AI, and towns are trying to keep it out
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Trump walked back the proposed OpenAI stake on camera even as bettors still price it as likely, and the physical AI buildout is now running into local moratoriums.
item.content:encoded:
Trump walked back the proposed OpenAI stake on camera even as bettors still price it as likely, and the physical AI buildout is now running into local moratoriums.

The AI story turned into a government story overnight, and it cut both ways. Washington is circling equity in the labs while local governments start slamming the door on the data centers. Both are worth more attention than the holiday-thin headlines suggest.

1. Trump walked back the OpenAI stake on camera, but the market still bets it happens. Yesterday the framing was concrete, a proposed 5% government stake in OpenAI worth about $42.5B. On CNBC overnight, per the People to follow list, Trump would not confirm it and pointed instead to the 10% stake the government already holds in Intel. Yet Polymarket still prices a US government equity stake in OpenAI at 57%, and Sam Altman is on record calling public ownership the best way to share AI's upside, in an arrangement that reportedly would have pulled in other labs too.

The specifics are soft and the president is hedging in public. The direction is not soft at all. The idea of the state taking direct equity in frontier AI has moved from unthinkable to a coin-flip bet in a matter of days.

2. The AI buildout is hitting the one wall money cannot move, local government. The counterweight to all that federal enthusiasm is showing up in county commission meetings. Blackstone abandoned a Virginia data center, and roughly 20 Florida counties are weighing data-center moratoriums, per Polymarket. This is the part of the AI story that does not live on a chart. The buildout needs land, water, and enormous amounts of power, and the people who live next to it are starting to vote on it. When the physical footprint of the boom becomes a ballot-box issue, that is a real constraint on the timeline, not a talking point.

3. Around the world, quickly.

  • Republicans firmed up their Senate edge. On Kalshi, the GOP is now 61% to hold the Senate, up from the high 50s a day earlier. Slow, quiet drift in the party-control market is usually more real than any single poll.
  • The recession trade got unwound. Odds of a US recession this year fell to about 8%, an all-time low on Kalshi, even after a soft jobs print. The market is pricing a slowdown that keeps not arriving.
  • The world's assets hit a new high. Global market capitalization set a record near $166T. Whatever the AI-bubble argument does to individual names, the aggregate pile of wealth has never been larger. That is its own kind of risk when sentiment turns.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Michael Burry's Micron short, the semiconductor flush, Bitcoin's contested bottom, and Tesla's delivery-day flush are all market mechanics and live in Investing. The AI-bubble bear case (Meta's compute sale, the China cost curve, OpenAI's burn rate) is the big Investing story this morning. The revolt over Fable 5's guardrails and the week's model releases are covered in AI & Tools. Nothing on the news side was dropped as noise today.

item.title: The Supreme Court just loosened the campaign-money rules, and the president disclosed $2 billion
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: SCOTUS loosened the limits on party spending, Trump reported more than $2B in income with most of it from crypto, and the layoff data says AI is now the No. 1 reason Americans are losing jobs.
item.content:encoded:
SCOTUS loosened the limits on party spending, Trump reported more than $2B in income with most of it from crypto, and the layoff data says AI is now the No. 1 reason Americans are losing jobs.

Two stories actually moved today, and both are about money and power flowing to the top. Everything else was noise or a market story wearing a news headline. Here is what mattered.

1. The Supreme Court loosened the limits on party spending.
